title: Weekly Flight Performance Analysis
text: The total number of flights operated varies significantly by day of the week. Some days have higher travel demand while others are quieter. This section examines day-of-week flight volumes and rolling flight trends across September 2019.

title: Weekly Flight Performance Analysis
text:
Day-of-Week Flight Volume Rankings

Table 2 — Rank of Day in Week by Number of Flights Performed



Day of the Week
Number of Flights
Rank




Monday
106,461
1


Sunday
99,427
2


Thursday
85,135
3


Friday
85,095
4


Wednesday
81,677
5


Tuesday
81,150
6


Saturday
67,034
7



Summary Statistics:

Mean flights/day: 87,711.29
Total flights in September 2019: 605,979


Key Finding: Monday was the single busiest day with over 106,000 flights — nearly 59% more than the quietest day, Saturday (67,034 flights). Monday and Sunday together account for a disproportionate share of weekly traffic, reflecting weekend travel patterns where passengers depart Sunday and return Monday.

title: Weekly Flight Performance Analysis
text:
Flights Over Preceding Three Days
The line chart below shows the total flights on each date, aggregated over a rolling 3-day window. The first three days (Sep 1–3) are excluded as they lack sufficient preceding history.



Analysis of Flights Over Preceding Three Days. Red = max, yellow = min, blue dashed = mean.



Key Finding: The plot reveals a repeating 5-day pattern. Notably, September 11, 18, and 25 (all Wednesdays) show sharp spikes in the 3-day rolling total. This is because these Wednesdays capture the cumulative flights of Sunday + Monday + Tuesday — the three busiest days of the week — in their preceding window.

title: Air Traffic On-Time Performance Analysis
text:
📄 Read the Full Report



The complete project report is available as a PDF.
Read in browser   ⬇ Download PDF

title: Air Traffic On-Time Performance Analysis
text:
Overview
This project examines U.S. domestic airline on-time performance data from September 2019 using MySQL for data querying and R for visualization. The analysis covers three major topics: airline and airport departure delay patterns, weekly flight volume trends, and flight cancellation reasons.
On-time performance data contains records of flights by date, airline, originating airport, destination airport, and many other operational details. The goal is to identify which airlines and airports perform poorly, which days of the week are busiest, and what drives flight cancellations.

title: Air Traffic On-Time Performance Analysis
text:
Abstract
Numerous flights operate every day across hundreds of destinations in the United States, and unexpected schedule changes occur for a wide variety of reasons — weather events, carrier issues, and national air system disruptions. With real-time flight data from September 1–30, 2019, this project examines a dataset of 605,979 flights across 17 airlines and 285 airports to answer key operational questions about airline performance.
The first topic is airline and airport departure analysis. Airlines vary significantly in their worst-case delay performance, with Envoy Air recording a maximum departure delay of 1,753 minutes and Southwest Airlines recording the shortest at 438 minutes. Airport-level analysis reveals that Southwest Oregon Regional Airport had the highest average departure delay at 43.92 minutes.
The second topic is weekly flight performance analysis. Monday was the busiest day of the week with 106,461 flights, while Saturday had the fewest at 67,034. A rolling three-day flight count analysis shows a repeating five-day cycle, with Wednesdays consistently producing the highest cumulative totals due to the preceding Sunday and Monday peaks.
The third topic is cancellation reason analysis. Of 605,979 total flights, 10,016 were cancelled — a rate of 1.7%. Weather was the dominant cancellation cause at 86.2%, consistent with September being peak hurricane season in the United States. The most-affected airports are concentrated in the southern states, where severe weather conditions are most prevalent during this period.

title: Cancellation Reason Analysis
text: Beyond delays, some flights are cancelled entirely. This section examines how many flights were cancelled, the reasons behind them, and which airports were most affected during September 2019.

title: Cancellation Reason Analysis
text:
Overall Cancellation Rate

Table 3 — Cancelled vs. Operated Flights



Flight Status
Number of Flights
Percentage




Operated
595,964
98.3%


Cancelled
10,016
1.7%




Key Finding: Of 605,979 total scheduled flights, 10,016 were cancelled — a cancellation rate of 1.7%. While this is a relatively low figure, it still represents over 10,000 disrupted journeys within a single month.

title: Cancellation Reason Analysis
text:
Cancellation Reasons Breakdown
The pie chart below breaks down the reasons for all 10,016 cancellations in September 2019.



Pie Chart of Cancellation Reasons for September 2019.



Key Finding: Weather was overwhelmingly the dominant cause of cancellations:

Weather: 86.2%
National Air System: 9.9%
Carrier: 3.9%

This is consistent with September being peak hurricane season in the United States. Storms and severe weather events concentrated in the southern and coastal states drove the majority of cancellations.

title: Cancellation Reason Analysis
text:
Top 10 Airports by Cancellation Count
The bar chart below shows the 10 airports (out of 285 total) with the most cancellations during September 2019.



Top 10 Airports by Cancellation Count (all reasons).



Key Finding: The airports with the most cancellations are predominantly located in the southern United States — a region directly in the path of hurricane-season weather systems. This geographic pattern further supports the outsized role of weather as the primary cancellation driver in September 2019.

title: Code & Methods
text: This page documents all MySQL queries and R visualization code used to produce the results in this analysis.

title: Code & Methods
text:
R Code

Data Loading
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

d1 <- read.csv("project_1.csv")   # Max delay by airline
d2 <- read.csv("project_2.csv")   # Max early departure by airline
d3 <- read.csv("project_3.csv")   # Day-of-week flight counts
d4 <- read.csv("project_4.csv")   # Highest avg delay airport
d5 <- read.csv("project_5.csv")   # Highest avg delay airport per airline
d6 <- read.csv("project_6b.csv")  # Cancellation counts by airport
d7 <- read.csv("project_7.csv")   # Rolling 3-day flight counts



Figure 1 — Maximum Delay Minutes by Airline
plot1 <- ggplot(d1, aes(x = reorder(Airline, -MaxDelayMinutes), y = MaxDelayMinutes)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "skyblue") +
  geom_point(aes(x = max_airline, y = max_delay), color = "red", size = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(x = min_airline, y = min_delay), color = "blue", size = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mean_delay, color = "black", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Maximum Delay Minutes by Airline",
    x = "Airline", y = "Maximum Delay Minutes", caption = "Figure 1"
  ) +
  annotate("text", x = max_airline, y = max_delay + 100,
           label = paste("Max:", max_delay), color = "red", size = 2) +
  annotate("text", x = min_airline, y = min_delay - 100,
           label = paste("Min:", min_delay), color = "blue", size = 2) +
  annotate("text", x = 2, y = mean_delay + 100,
           label = paste("Mean:", round(mean_delay, 1)), color = "black", hjust = 0.5, size = 2) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 60, hjust = 1),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA)
  )

ggsave("my_plot1.png", plot = plot1, width = 6, height = 6)



Figure 2 — Maximum Early Departure Minutes by Airline
summary_data <- d2 %>%
  mutate(EarlyDepartureMinutes = abs(EarlyDepartureMinutes)) %>%
  summarise(
    Max = max(EarlyDepartureMinutes),
    Min = min(EarlyDepartureMinutes),
    Mean = mean(EarlyDepartureMinutes)
  )

plot2b <- ggplot(d2, aes(x = reorder(Airline, abs(EarlyDepartureMinutes)),
                          y = abs(EarlyDepartureMinutes))) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "skyblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_text(aes(label = abs(EarlyDepartureMinutes)), hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Maximum Early Departure Minutes by Airline",
    x = "Airline", y = "Early Departure in Minutes", caption = "Figure 2"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 60, hjust = 1),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA)
  )

ggsave("my_plot2b.png", plot = plot2b, width = 6, height = 6)



Figure 4 — Airport Location for Highest Average Departure Delay
library(maps)
library(ggrepel)

lat1  <- 43
long1 <- -124
air1  <- d4
air1$Latitude  <- lat1
air1$Longitude <- long1

usa_map <- map_data("state")

plot4 <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = usa_map, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group),
               fill = "lightblue", color = "white") +
  geom_point(data = air1, aes(x = Longitude, y = Latitude),
             color = "red", size = 2) +
  geom_text_repel(data = air1,
                  aes(x = Longitude, y = Latitude,
                      label = paste(AirportName, AvgDelay, sep = "\n")),
                  size = 3, box.padding = 0.5, point.padding = 0.5,
                  max.overlaps = 50) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Airport Location for Highest Average Departure Delay",
    x = "Longitude", y = "Latitude", caption = "Figure 4"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA)
  )

ggsave("my_plot4.png", plot = plot4, width = 7, height = 7)



Figure 5 — Airports with Highest Average Departure Delay per Airline
library(ggrepel)
library(usmap)

airport           <- d5
airport$latitude  <- a11$Latitude
airport$longitude <- a11$Longitude

plot5 <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = usa_map, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group),
               fill = "lightblue", color = "white") +
  geom_point(data = airport, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude),
             color = "red", size = 2) +
  geom_text_repel(data = airport,
                  aes(x = longitude, y = latitude,
                      label = paste(AirportName, AirlineName, sep = "\n")),
                  size = 2, box.padding = 0.5, point.padding = 0.5,
                  max.overlaps = 50) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Airport Location with Highest Average Departure Delays by Airlines",
    x = "Longitude", y = "Latitude", caption = "Figure 6"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA)
  )

ggsave("my_plot5.png", plot = plot5, width = 9, height = 7)



Figure 5c — Bar Chart of Highest Average Delay Airport per Airline
d5 <- d5 %>%
  mutate(
    Highlight = case_when(
      AvgDelay == max(AvgDelay) ~ "Max",
      AvgDelay == min(AvgDelay) ~ "Min",
      TRUE ~ "Normal"
    )
  )

plot5c <- ggplot(d5, aes(x = reorder(AirlineName, -AvgDelay),
                          y = AvgDelay, fill = AirportName)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", aes(alpha = Highlight)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(AvgDelay, 1)), vjust = -0.5, size = 4.5) +
  scale_alpha_manual(values = c("Max" = 1, "Min" = 1, "Normal" = 0.8), guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Airports with Highest Average Departure Delay for Each Airline",
    x = "Airline", y = "Average Delays (minutes)",
    fill = "Airport", caption = "Figure 5"
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 5)
  )

ggsave("my_plot5c.png", plot = plot5c, width = 10, height = 7)



Figure 7 — Pie Chart of Cancellation Reasons
overall_reasons <- most_frequent_reasons %>%
  group_by(reason) %>%
  summarise(total_cancelcount = sum(total_cancelcount)) %>%
  mutate(percentage = total_cancelcount / sum(total_cancelcount) * 100)

plot6 <- ggplot(overall_reasons, aes(x = "", y = total_cancelcount, fill = reason)) +
  geom_bar(width = 1, stat = "identity") +
  coord_polar("y", start = 0) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(percentage, 1), "%")),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5)) +
  labs(title = "Pie Chart of Cancellation Reasons", caption = "Figure 7") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA)
  )

ggsave("my_plot6.png", plot = plot6, width = 9, height = 7)



Figure 8 — Top 10 Airports by Cancellation Count
top_cancellation_airports <- d6 %>%
  group_by(AirportName) %>%
  summarise(cancelcount = sum(cancelcount)) %>%
  arrange(desc(cancelcount)) %>%
  slice(1:10)

plot6b <- ggplot(top_cancellation_airports,
                 aes(x = reorder(AirportName, -cancelcount), y = cancelcount)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "orange") +
  labs(
    title = "Top 10 Airports by Cancellation Counts (All Reasons)",
    x = "Airport Name", y = "Number of Cancellations", caption = "Figure 8"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 65, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold")
  )

ggsave("my_plot6b.png", plot = plot6b, width = 9, height = 7)



Plot 1 — Flights Over Preceding Three Days
d7$FlightDate <- as.Date(d7$FlightDate)
max_flights  <- d7[which.max(d7$flights_over_preceding_three_days), ]
min_flights  <- d7[which.min(d7$flights_over_preceding_three_days), ]
mean_flights <- mean(d7$flights_over_preceding_three_days)

plot7 <- ggplot(d7, aes(x = FlightDate, y = flights_over_preceding_three_days)) +
  geom_line(aes(color = "Trend Line"), size = 1) +
  geom_point(data = max_flights,
             aes(x = FlightDate, y = flights_over_preceding_three_days, color = "Max Flights"),
             size = 4) +
  geom_point(data = min_flights,
             aes(x = FlightDate, y = flights_over_preceding_three_days, color = "Min Flights"),
             size = 4) +
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = mean_flights, color = "Mean Flights"),
             linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Trend Line"  = "orange",
    "Max Flights" = "red",
    "Min Flights" = "yellow",
    "Mean Flights" = "blue"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Analysis of Flights Over Preceding Three Days",
    x = "Flight Date", y = "Number of Flights",
    color = "Legend", caption = "Plot 1"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_date(breaks = seq(min(d7$FlightDate), max(d7$FlightDate), by = "1 day")) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = "black")
  )

ggsave("my_plot7.png", plot = plot7, width = 9, height = 7)

title: Code & Methods
text:
MySQL Queries

Query 1 — Maximum Departure Delay by Airline
SELECT 
    Name AS Airline,
    MAX(DepDelay) AS MaxDelayMinutes
FROM al_perf
JOIN L_AIRLINE_ID 
    ON al_perf.DOT_ID_Reporting_Airline = L_AIRLINE_ID.ID
GROUP BY Name
ORDER BY MaxDelayMinutes DESC;
-- 17 rows returned



Query 2 — Maximum Early Departure by Airline
SELECT 
    Name AS Airline,
    MIN(DepDelay) AS EarlyDepartureMinutes
FROM al_perf
JOIN L_AIRLINE_ID 
    ON al_perf.DOT_ID_Reporting_Airline = L_AIRLINE_ID.ID
GROUP BY Name
ORDER BY EarlyDepartureMinutes ASC;
-- 17 rows returned



Query 3 — Flight Count Ranked by Day of Week
SELECT 
    L_WEEKDAYS.Day AS Day,
    COUNT(*) AS NumberOfFlights,
    RANK() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) AS Ranking
FROM al_perf
JOIN L_WEEKDAYS ON al_perf.DayOfWeek = L_WEEKDAYS.Code
GROUP BY DayOfWeek
ORDER BY NumberOfFlights DESC;
-- 7 rows returned



Query 4 — Airport with Highest Average Departure Delay
WITH AirportDelays AS (
    SELECT 
        s.OriginAirportID AS AirportID,
        AVG(s.DepDelayMinutes) AS AvgDelay
    FROM al_perf s
    GROUP BY s.OriginAirportID
)
SELECT 
    a.Name AS AirportName,
    a.ID   AS AirportCode,
    d.AvgDelay
FROM AirportDelays d
JOIN L_AIRPORT_ID a ON d.AirportID = a.ID
ORDER BY d.AvgDelay DESC
LIMIT 1;
-- 1 row returned



Query 5 — Highest Average Delay Airport per Airline
WITH airlineairportdelay AS (
    SELECT 
        s.DOT_ID_Reporting_Airline AS AirlineID,
        s.OriginAirportID          AS AirportID,
        AVG(s.DepDelayMinutes)     AS AvgDelay
    FROM al_perf s
    GROUP BY s.DOT_ID_Reporting_Airline, s.OriginAirportID
),
maxdelayperairline AS (
    SELECT d.AirlineID, d.AirportID, d.AvgDelay
    FROM airlineairportdelay d
    JOIN (
        SELECT AirlineID, MAX(AvgDelay) AS MaxDelay
        FROM airlineairportdelay
        GROUP BY AirlineID
    ) m ON d.AirlineID = m.AirlineID AND d.AvgDelay = m.MaxDelay
)
SELECT 
    a.Name AS AirlineName,
    p.Name AS AirportName,
    m.AvgDelay
FROM maxdelayperairline m
JOIN L_AIRLINE_ID a ON m.AirlineID = a.ID
JOIN L_AIRPORT_ID p ON m.AirportID = p.ID;
-- 17 rows returned



Query 6a — Count of Cancelled Flights
SELECT * FROM al_perf
WHERE Cancelled = 1;
-- 10,016 rows returned



Query 6b — Most Frequent Cancellation Reason per Airport
WITH cancel AS (
    SELECT 
        s.OriginAirportID AS airportID,
        c.Reason          AS reason,
        COUNT(*)          AS cancelcount
    FROM al_perf s
    JOIN L_CANCELATION c ON s.CancellationCode = c.Code
    WHERE s.Cancelled = 1
    GROUP BY s.OriginAirportID, c.Reason
),
frequentreason AS (
    SELECT airportID, reason, cancelcount
    FROM cancel
    WHERE cancelcount = (
        SELECT MAX(cancelcount)
        FROM cancel cc
        WHERE cc.airportID = cancel.airportID
    )
)
SELECT 
    a.Name AS AirportName,
    m.reason,
    m.cancelcount
FROM frequentreason m
JOIN L_AIRPORT_ID a ON m.airportID = a.ID;
-- 285 rows returned



Query 7 — Flights Over Preceding Three Days
WITH countflight AS (
    SELECT FlightDate, COUNT(*) AS dayflights
    FROM al_perf
    GROUP BY FlightDate
),
averageflightbyday AS (
    SELECT 
        c1.FlightDate,
        CASE 
            WHEN COUNT(c1.dayflights) < 3 THEN NULL
            ELSE AVG(c2.dayflights)
        END AS flights_over_preceding_three_days
    FROM countflight c1
    LEFT JOIN countflight c2
        ON c2.FlightDate BETWEEN DATE_SUB(c1.FlightDate, INTERVAL 3 DAY)
                              AND DATE_SUB(c1.FlightDate, INTERVAL 1 DAY)
    GROUP BY c1.FlightDate
)
SELECT FlightDate, flights_over_preceding_three_days
FROM averageflightbyday
WHERE flights_over_preceding_three_days IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY FlightDate;
-- 27 rows returned

title: Airline & Airport Departure Analysis
text: This section examines departure delay patterns across airlines and airports during September 2019. It is common for airlines to depart later than expected — or even early — due to various operational and environmental factors.

title: Airline & Airport Departure Analysis
text:
1. Maximum Departure Delay by Airline
Airlines differ widely in their worst-case delay performance. The chart below ranks all 17 airlines from longest to shortest maximum departure delay recorded in September 2019.



Maximum Delay Minutes by Airline. Red dot = max, Blue dot = min, dashed line = mean.



Key Finding: Envoy Air had the longest single departure delay at 1,753 minutes, while Southwest Airlines had the shortest maximum delay at 438 minutes. The mean of all airlines’ maximum delays was 1,150.9 minutes.
Note: these are maximum delays — not averages — so even airlines that generally perform well can appear here due to one exceptional event.

title: Airline & Airport Departure Analysis
text:
2. Maximum Early Departure by Airline
Flights can also depart earlier than scheduled. The chart below shows the maximum early departure (in absolute minutes) for each airline.



Maximum Early Departure Minutes by Airline.



Key Finding: Republic Airlines had the greatest early departure at 62 minutes ahead of schedule. Hawaiian Airlines had the smallest maximum early departure at just 22 minutes early.

title: Airline & Airport Departure Analysis
section: 3. Airport with Highest Average Departure Delay
text:
3. Airport with Highest Average Departure Delay
While the analyses above focused on individual airlines, airports also vary significantly in average departure performance.

Table 1 — Airport with Highest Average Departure Delay








Airport Name
Airport Code
Average Delay (minutes)




North Bend/Coos Bay, OR: Southwest Oregon Regional
13694
43.92




Key Finding: Southwest Oregon Regional Airport (North Bend/Coos Bay, OR) had the highest average departure delay of approximately 43.92 minutes among all airports in the dataset.

The map below shows this airport’s location in Oregon, USA.



Location of the airport with the highest average departure delay.

title: Airline & Airport Departure Analysis
section: 4. Highest Average Departure Delay Airport per Airline
text:
4. Highest Average Departure Delay Airport per Airline
Looking one level deeper: for each airline, which single airport produced the highest average departure delay?



Airports with Highest Average Departure Delay for Each Airline.



Key Finding: Most airlines’ worst-airport average delays were under 100 minutes. However, two clear outliers stand out:

Endeavor Air at General Mitchell International (Milwaukee): 360.5 min average delay
SkyWest Airlines at Philadelphia International: 167 min average delay

At the other end, Southwest Airlines at Newark Liberty International had the lowest airline-specific average delay at just 11.2 minutes, suggesting strong operational reliability across all airports.

The map below shows the geographic distribution of these worst-performing airports for each airline.



Locations of airports with highest average departure delays by airline.
